cities.name: Ahmedabad
cities.overview: Ahmedabad, Gujarat's largest city and former capital, is a vibrant UNESCO World Heritage City that blends 600 years of history with modern industry. Famous for its pols (traditional wooden havelis), stepwells, mosques, and the Sabarmati Riverfront, it was Mahatma Gandhi's base during the freedom struggle and remains the cultural and commercial heart of Gujarat.
cities.history: Founded in 1411 by Sultan Ahmed Shah, Ahmedabad served as the capital of the Gujarat Sultanate. It became a textile hub in the 19th century, earning the nickname 'Manchester of the East'. The Sabarmati Ashram was Gandhi's home from 1917 to 1930, from where he launched the Dandi March in 1930.

places.name: Sabarmati Ashram
places.description: Mahatma Gandhi's former residence and headquarters of the Indian independence movement. The museum houses original letters, photographs, and personal belongings of the Mahatma.
places.tips: Visit early morning for a peaceful experience. Attend the evening prayer meeting if scheduled.

places.name: Sidi Saiyyed Mosque
places.description: Famous for its exquisite jali (stone lattice) windows depicting the Tree of Life. Built in 1573, it is one of the finest examples of Indo-Islamic architecture in Gujarat.
places.tips: Best light for photography is in the late afternoon. The mosque is still in use — dress modestly.

places.name: Adalaj Stepwell
places.description: A stunning five-storey stepwell built in 1499 with intricate carvings of flowers, leaves, and mythological scenes. One of the most beautiful stepwells in India.
places.tips: Visit in the morning to avoid crowds. The play of light and shadows inside is magical.

places.name: Kankaria Lake
places.description: A scenic artificial lake with a 5.5km promenade, zoo, amusement park, and musical fountain. Popular with families and locals for evening walks.
places.tips: Evening visits are best for the fountain show and lights. Great street food options around the lake.

places.name: Sabarmati Riverfront
places.description: A 11km long developed riverfront with parks, amphitheatres, and viewing galleries. Offers beautiful sunset views and hosts cultural events.
places.tips: Walk or cycle along the promenade at sunset. Visit the nearby flower garden and library.

places.name: Hutheesing Jain Temple
places.description: A magnificent 19th-century Jain temple built entirely of white marble with 52 intricately carved shrines.
places.tips: Remove shoes and leather items before entering. Photography is not allowed inside the main shrine.

cities.name: Vadodara
cities.overview: Vadodara, also known as Baroda, is Gujarat's cultural capital. Home to the magnificent Laxmi Vilas Palace and a rich legacy of art, music, and architecture from the Gaekwad dynasty, it offers a perfect blend of royal heritage and modern vibrancy.
cities.history: Ruled by the Gaekwad dynasty for over 200 years, Vadodara flourished as a centre of arts and education. Maharaja Sayajirao Gaekwad III transformed it into a modern city with museums, libraries, and the famous Laxmi Vilas Palace, one of the largest private residences in the world.

places.name: Laxmi Vilas Palace
places.description: One of the largest private residences in the world, built in 1890 in Indo-Saracenic style. The palace grounds include a zoo, museum, and golf course.
places.tips: Guided tours available. The Durbar Hall and museum are highlights.

places.name: Maharaja Fatehsingh Museum
places.description: Housed in the former Baroda Museum building, it showcases a superb collection of European paintings, Indian miniatures, and royal artifacts.
places.tips: Excellent collection of Raja Ravi Varma paintings. Combine with palace visit.

cities.name: Dwarka
cities.overview: Dwarka is one of the Char Dham and Sapta Puri pilgrimage sites, believed to be the ancient kingdom of Lord Krishna. The majestic Dwarkadhish Temple stands on the Arabian Sea coast, attracting millions of devotees every year.
cities.history: According to Hindu mythology, Krishna established his kingdom here after leaving Mathura. The present temple dates to the 15th–16th century, though the site has been a sacred centre for over 5,000 years.

places.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
places.description: The main temple dedicated to Lord Krishna. Its 5-tiered shikhara rises 43m high with a flag that changes 5 times a day.
places.tips: Non-Hindus can view from outside certain areas. Evening aarti is spectacular.

cities.name: Somnath
cities.overview: Somnath is home to one of the 12 Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva. The temple, rebuilt multiple times after invasions, stands majestically on the Arabian Sea coast and is a symbol of resilience and faith.
cities.history: The original temple was built in 649 AD and destroyed and rebuilt several times. The present structure was inaugurated in 1951. It has been attacked at least 17 times in history.

places.name: Somnath Temple
places.description: One of the most revered Jyotirlingas, built in Chalukya style with intricate carvings. The light-and-sound show in the evening narrates its history.
places.tips: Attend the evening aarti and light-and-sound show. Photography inside main sanctum is restricted.

cities.name: Sasan Gir
cities.tagline: Last Refuge of the Asiatic Lion
cities.overview: Sasan Gir is the only place in the world where Asiatic lions roam free in the wild. The dry deciduous forest is also home to leopards, deer, and over 300 bird species.
cities.history: Declared a wildlife sanctuary in 1965 and national park in 1975, Gir saved the Asiatic lion from extinction. The population has grown from 20 in the 1930s to over 600 today.

places.name: Gir National Park Safari
places.description: Jeep safari through the dry deciduous forest to spot Asiatic lions, leopards, spotted deer, and crocodiles.
places.tips: Book safari permits online in advance. Early morning safaris have better sightings.

cities.name: Bhuj
cities.overview: Bhuj is the historic capital of Kutch, known for its palaces, museums, handicrafts, and as the gateway to the Rann of Kutch. The town showcases rich Kutchi culture, embroidery, and resilience after the 2001 earthquake.
cities.history: Once the capital of the princely state of Kutch, Bhuj was devastated by the 2001 earthquake but has been beautifully rebuilt. The Aina Mahal and Prag Mahal are surviving gems of royal architecture.

places.name: Aina Mahal
places.description: 18th-century palace with exquisite mirror work and European-style interiors. A masterpiece of Kutchi craftsmanship.
places.tips: Guided tours explain the history beautifully.

cities.name: Rann of Kutch
cities.overview: The Rann of Kutch is a vast seasonal salt marsh that turns into a shimmering white desert during winter. Famous for the Rann Utsav cultural festival, star-filled skies, and unique handicrafts of the region.
cities.history: Once part of the Arabian Sea, the Rann became a salt desert due to tectonic shifts. It has been a cultural melting pot of Hindu, Muslim, and tribal communities for centuries.

places.name: White Rann
places.description: The vast salt flat that glows white under moonlight. The Rann Utsav features tent cities, folk performances, and handicraft villages.
places.tips: Visit during full moon for the magical experience. Book tent stays well in advance.

cities.name: Kevadia
cities.tagline: Home of the Statue of Unity
cities.overview: Kevadia is home to the world's tallest statue — the Statue of Unity — honouring Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel. Surrounded by the Narmada River and lush hills, it offers adventure activities, museums, and eco-tourism.
cities.history: Developed as a major tourism hub around the Statue of Unity, inaugurated in 2018 on the 143rd birth anniversary of Sardar Patel.

places.name: Statue of Unity
places.description: The 182m tall statue of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, the tallest in the world. Includes a viewing gallery at 153m height with panoramic views.
places.tips: Book tickets online. The light-and-sound show in the evening is impressive.

cities.name: Saputara
cities.overview: Saputara is Gujarat's only hill station, nestled in the Sahyadri range. With misty hills, lakes, and waterfalls, it offers a cool retreat and adventure activities like boating and trekking.
cities.history: Developed as a tourist hill station in the 1980s by Gujarat Tourism. The name means 'serpent's abode' due to its snake-like hills.

places.name: Saputara Lake
places.description: A serene lake ideal for boating and pedal boating with surrounding hills and forests.
places.tips: Evening boat rides are romantic. Visit the nearby Artist Village.

cities.name: Palitana
cities.overview: Palitana is the world's largest cluster of Jain temples, with over 900 marble temples on Shatrunjaya Hill. A major Jain pilgrimage site requiring a steep climb of over 3,500 steps.
cities.history: The temples date back to the 11th century. Shatrunjaya hill is considered one of the most sacred Jain sites, where 23 of the 24 Tirthankaras attained enlightenment.

places.name: Shatrunjaya Hill Temples
places.description: Cluster of over 900 Jain temples atop a 600m hill. The climb offers breathtaking views and spiritual serenity.
places.tips: Start early morning. Palanquins available for elderly pilgrims.

cities.name: Junagadh
cities.overview: Junagadh is famous for its ancient Buddhist caves, the majestic Girnar Hill with Jain and Hindu temples, and the historic Uparkot Fort.
cities.history: Ruled by various dynasties including the Mauryas and later the Nawabs of Junagadh. The rock edicts of Emperor Ashoka are still preserved here.

places.name: Girnar Hill Temples
places.description: A sacred hill with 10,000 steps leading to Jain and Hindu temples. Offers panoramic views of Junagadh.
places.tips: Start before dawn for the climb. Palanquins available.

cities.name: Patan
cities.overview: Patan is famous for the UNESCO World Heritage Site Rani ki Vav, one of the most intricate stepwells in India, and the ancient Sahastra Linga Talav.
cities.history: Capital of the Solanki dynasty (942–1244 AD). Rani ki Vav was built in the 11th century by Queen Udayamati.

places.name: Rani ki Vav
places.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site — a marvel of 11th-century stepwell architecture with over 500 intricate sculptures.
places.tips: Best visited in winter when water levels are low to see all carvings.

cities.name: Diu
cities.overview: Diu is a former Portuguese colony known for its pristine beaches, Portuguese forts, and laid-back island vibe. A perfect blend of history, beaches, and water sports.
cities.history: Under Portuguese rule until 1961. The Diu Fort and churches reflect strong Portuguese influence.

places.name: Diu Fort
places.description: 16th-century Portuguese fort overlooking the Arabian Sea. Offers stunning sea views and cannons.
places.tips: Sunset views are spectacular.

cities.name: Porbandar
cities.overview: Porbandar is the birthplace of Mahatma Gandhi. The Kirti Mandir and ancestral home of Gandhi offer deep insights into the life of the Father of the Nation.
cities.history: Ancient port city mentioned in the Mahabharata. Gandhi was born here in 1869 in the family home that is now a museum.

places.name: Kirti Mandir
places.description: Memorial to Mahatma Gandhi built on the site of his ancestral home. Includes a library and museum.
places.tips: Peaceful and inspiring place to reflect on Gandhi's life.

cities.name: Jamnagar
cities.overview: Jamnagar is known for its beautiful lakes, the historic Lakhota Palace, and as the world's largest producer of brass. It also serves as a base for marine national park visits.
cities.history: Founded in 1540 by Jam Rawal. The city is famous for its 19th-century palaces and the vibrant Bandhani tie-dye textiles.

places.name: Lakhota Lake & Palace
places.description: A scenic lake in the heart of the city with a 19th-century palace museum on an island.
places.tips: Beautiful for evening walks and birdwatching.
